sites.name: Rhoneymeade
sites.description: ClearWater Conservancy's first conservation easement. The roughly 150-acre site was first settled by Michael Rhone in the late 1700s and has been continuously farmed for over 200 years. A brick farmhouse, finished in 1853 by Michael Rhone's grandson Leonard Rhone (originator of the Grange Fair), is near the center of the property. The farm was purchased by Richard Morgan, a retired Penn State professor, in 1984, who donated the conservation easement in 1986 and placed the farmhouse on the national register of historic places. Currently owned and managed by Rhoneymeade Inc., a nonprofit formed in 1989 focused on connecting people, art, nature and agriculture. Open to the public seasonally during daylight hours.

sites.name: Nittany Farms
sites.description: In 1990, on the 150th anniversary of construction of the stone farmhouse on Nittany Farms, Mr. Galen Dreibelbis donated a conservation easement on his 287-acre beef cattle farm. The property reaches from Route 322 to Route 45, bounded on the east by Sharer Road. Beyond protecting productive farm soils in a high growth area, the easement protects 3,700 linear feet of Spring Creek, only 1.5 miles from the eastern-most headwater springs of the main stem of Spring Creek, and the headwaters of Cedar Run. Protection of Nittany Farms was the first of many steps ClearWater has taken to protect a 'Ribbon of Green' along the Spring Creek corridor. Cattle still graze the property today, though a very old cemetery (Spring Creek Cemetery / Cedar Creek Graveyard) has been reclaimed by the hillside forest.

sites.name: Millbrook Marsh Fen
sites.description: Gifted to ClearWater in 2000 by Doris M. Nieble, after initial discussions with the Northcentral Pennsylvania Conservancy. The Fen is part of Millbrook Marsh (a calcareous marsh), listed as the only sizable open sedge fen in Centre County. While as many as ten species of special concern were reported in the County's 1996 Natural Heritage Inventory Report, only three species were reported in the 2002 NHI (Bebb's sedge, the prairie sedge, and the slender wedge). Management is addressed in the Protection and Management Plan for the Millbrook Marsh, with a 2011 Addendum prepared by William Hilshey and Rob Brooks. Classified as a Preservation Sub-Zone of the Wetland-Riparian Zone of Millbrook Marsh. Boardwalks, paths, and all structural intrusions are not permitted. Only carefully controlled scientific research and monitoring may be permitted if consistent with ecological preservation.

sites.name: Thompson Woods Preserve
sites.description: Conservation of this parcel was a step in the plan to connect Millbrook Marsh, the Duck Pond, Walnut Spring Park, Slab Cabin Park, and Lederer Park. Identified as the largest remaining forest (mixed hardwood, 8 to 90 years old) contiguous to State College Borough. Walnut Spring and Thompson Run cross the western portion of the property, helping protect Walnut Run, the Duck Pond, and Thompson Run from stormwater runoff, and buffering Thompson Meadow Spring. ClearWater raised the funds for acquisition and holds the conservation easement. College Township (the landowner) and Centre Region Parks and Recreation are responsible for management. Any change in ownership or land use must be approved by DCNR.

sites.name: Millbrook Marsh
sites.description: In 1994, Penn State University decided Farm 12 was no longer needed. Adjacent marsh owners donated the marsh to PSU. In 1997, the Centre Region Recreation Authority entered a 35-year lease with PSU to establish a regional nature center at the renamed Millbrook Marsh. In 1998, a Management Plan was completed. In 2001, ClearWater accepted PSU's donation of a 35-year conservation easement on Millbrook Marsh. The property contains wetland and riparian conservation habitat. Slab Cabin Run (Cold Water Fishery) and Thompson Run (High Quality Cold Water Fishery) run through the property. The marsh serves as flood control, receives discharge from many springs, and its vegetation filters out sediments and contaminants. Three species of special concern are found in the marsh. Open to the public under management of the Centre Region Parks and Recreation Authority.

sites.name: Potter Farm
sites.description: This property has been in the Potter Family since it was first farmed by Fergus Potter in 1792 and became Centre County's first official Bicentennial Farm. Around 2001, Taylor Potter met with ClearWater to discuss a conservation easement. ClearWater developed a Supplemental Environmental Project (SEP) for the U.S. EPA's penalty settlement with Occidental Corporation related to cleanup of the Centre County Kepone Superfund Site. A partnership among ClearWater, the U.S. EPA, the Centre County Agricultural Land Preservation Program, the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service, and the PA Fish and Boat Commission used SEP funds for ClearWater to hold an 80-acre easement on upland forested and riparian areas, and for Centre County Ag Preservation to hold a 116-acre agricultural easement on the remaining tillable acreage. Contains unnamed tributaries to Cedar Run (a cold water fishery) and six wetland areas, including spring seeps and an interconnected wetland and stream complex.

sites.name: Hilton Garden Inn
sites.description: This property contains both riparian and wetland habitat suitable for conservation. The riparian habitat follows Thompson Run (a High Quality Cold Water Fishery) for 450 feet through the parcel. The wetland habitat is classified as palustrine, emergent, persistent, temporarily flooded (PEM1A) per NWI classification, and abuts Millbrook Marsh Fen at its northeastern border. It is part of the Millbrook Marsh Biological Diversity Area, one of the rarest community types in the county. ClearWater and Green Hotel Associates began discussions around 2001; the formal request for ClearWater to hold the easement came in August 2002. The ClearWater Board approved protection of 3.8 acres. College Township requires a 100-foot conservation buffer adjacent to the wetlands. The easement was signed in December 2004.

sites.name: Curtin Estate
sites.description: Located on the north side of Bald Eagle Mountain between Howard and Milesburg. Irish immigrant Roland Curtin founded the Eagle Iron Works here, with the Curtin family holdings eventually reaching 20,000 acres. The Pennsylvania Historical and Museum Commission later took control to restore Curtin Village. With one segment remaining, the Centre County Planning Commission investigated purchasing the property to protect Mount Eagle's water supply. ClearWater negotiated vigorously with the landowner over several years. Funding from the PA DCNR Land Trust Grant program, the Conservation Fund, and PA Department of Transportation propelled negotiations. At the October 2000 PA Game Commission meeting, approval was given to accept the 2,400-acre Curtin Estate from the Conservation Fund. In November 2004, the Conservation Fund transferred the property to the Pennsylvania Game Commission to become State Game Lands 323.

sites.name: Lisle
sites.description: An initial contact letter was sent to Ms. Lisle in 1994 as a landowner near Millbrook Marsh; no response came until Amos Goodall re-initiated contact in 2003. After a site visit, the Stewardship and Resources Committee recommended pursuing a conservation easement. Upon receiving grant funding for a Riparian Conservation Program, ClearWater pursued its first riparian conservation easement under the program on the Lisle property. The easement contributes to ClearWater's vision of a 'ribbon of green' along Spring Creek. The property contains over 600 feet of riparian habitat on the west side of Spring Creek, a High Quality Cold Water Fishery, and is home to hydrophytic plants such as blue lobelia and jewel weed.

sites.name: Eagle Field
sites.description: Discussions began in late 2003 between ClearWater Board Member Walt Ebaugh and landowner Karl Striedieck. The PA Game Commission ultimately provided grant funding for ClearWater to acquire the conservation easement. The property contains 190 acres of core forest, providing habitat for forest interior avian Species of Greatest Conservation Concern per the Pennsylvania Comprehensive Wildlife Strategy, including Wood Thrush, Scarlet Tanager, and Worm-eating Warbler. It also includes portions of the headwaters of Buffalo Run (a major contributor to Spring Creek) and Halfmoon Creek (a major contributor to Spruce Creek). Located on Bald Eagle Ridge, the property is an important flyway for raptors, with Golden Eagle counts among the highest recorded in eastern North America.

sites.name: Kettle Run Farm
sites.description: In November 2006, Katherine Alden approached ClearWater to discuss conserving her property. The ClearWater Board gave approval in January 2007, and by December 2007 the parties executed a donated conservation easement. The property protects water quality and wildlife habitat, especially forest habitat. The easement area includes the Kettle Run Biological Diversity Area containing an early successional field with lupine plants (Lupinus perennis), a spring feeding Kettle Run (a tributary of Penns Creek, a Cold Water Fishery), a palustrine emergent wetland for pollutant and sediment removal, and approximately 80 acres of mixed forest adjacent to Rothrock State Forest. The property also provides a buffer to Bald Eagle State Forest along a common 2,871-ft boundary.

sites.name: Buffalo Run Farm
sites.description: In 2001, Patton Township passed a $2.5 million open space funding program. The 2004 'Patton Township Open Space Plan' identified 94 potentially eligible properties; Buffalo Run Farm, owned by John and Barbara Fisher, was ranked the third highest priority for preservation. The property contains Exceptional Value wetlands and Buffalo Run, a High Quality Cold-Water Fishes stream classified as a wild trout stream. In 2007, the Fishers donated a conservation easement co-held by ClearWater Conservancy and Heritage Conservancy.

sites.name: Musser Gap
sites.description: In 2006 and 2007, ClearWater acquired nine separate parcels purchased or donated as a match for funds provided by PA DCNR through the Keystone Recreation, Park, and Conservation Fund Act. In August 2007, ClearWater conveyed the parcels to DCNR Bureau of Forestry to become part of Rothrock State Forest. Musser Gap is part of the Tussey Mountain Important Bird Area (designated by Pennsylvania Audubon), is adjacent to the Rothrock State Forest and Stone Mountain Important Bird Area, and is within the one-year zone of contribution of the State College Borough Water Authority's Harter and Thomas well fields, supplying drinking water to approximately 38,000 Centre Region residents.

sites.name: McCorkle Tree Farm
sites.description: In 2007, James McCorkle approached ClearWater about placing an easement on his property, co-held with his sisters Elizabeth Ann Jansson and Mary Lou Rozdilsky. A donated conservation easement was executed by December 2008. The property has been in the McCorkle family since 1940 and is used primarily for recreation including hiking, nature observation, fishing, gardening, and hunting. Seasonal cabins of the Spruce Creek Recreation Association are located in southern and eastern portions. The property has forest resources and early successional fields managed for wildlife diversity, with both a Forest Stewardship Plan and a Wildlife Habitat Management Plan in place. It is a Certified Tree Farm per the American Forest Foundation. Approximately 1,350 feet of Warriors Mark Run borders the north and northeast portions, with limestone rock outcroppings at the entrance and highest point.

sites.name: Tussey View Farm
sites.description: In Spring 2007, Fred Strouse approached ClearWater about conservation options. The property is within Central Mountains Important Mammal Area #20, Greater Tussey Mountain Important Bird Area #81, Galbraith Gap Run Headwaters Seep Biological Diversity Area, and contains the headwaters of the main stem of Spring Creek. ClearWater purchased a conservation easement on tax parcel 20-8-5 (189 acres) with a companion fee simple purchase of tax parcel 20-8-87 (71 acres) transferred to DCNR Bureau of Forestry. The eased property contains approximately 122 acres of mixed hardwood forest and 65 acres of early successional fields managed for wildlife diversity, and adds to the protection of large forest habitat on Tussey Mountain important for forest interior birds.

sites.name: Strouse
sites.description: Related to the Tussey View Farm project. In November 2009, ClearWater acquired this property from Fred Strouse and conveyed it to the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania DCNR Bureau of Forestry Rothrock State Forest.

sites.name: Barrens to Bald Eagle Wildlife Corridor
sites.description: ClearWater Conservancy purchased the B2BE Wildlife Corridor from RT Ventures in 2010. A Conservation Easement on the property is held by Halfmoon Township. The corridor creates a connection enabling safe passage for wildlife between State Game Land 176 and Bald Eagle Mountain, two ecologically significant forest blocks. Management has focused on enhancing habitat for vernal-pool breeding amphibians, deciduous or mixed forest birds, establishing pollinator habitat, and controlling invasive plant species. Open for public access with a gravel parking area, short walking trail, bench, and educational signs.

sites.name: Galbraith Gap
sites.description: ClearWater purchased 152 acres from Charles Aikens III and transferred the property to the PA DCNR Bureau of Forestry for incorporation into the adjacent Rothrock State Forest. The property contains an estimated 2,000 feet of an unnamed tributary to Galbraith Gap Run, one of five remaining headwater streams that sustain native brook trout in the Spring Creek Watershed. Galbraith Gap is also a forested mountain gap and a popular recreational access point into Rothrock State Forest.

sites.name: Buck Run
sites.description: Margaret Burgwin approached ClearWater Conservancy in 2010 with interest in donating a conservation easement on her 60-acre property. After investigation and due diligence, ClearWater and Ms. Burgwin, in partnership with Trout Unlimited, executed the conservation easement in January 2011. The property contains approximately 1,300 linear feet of Buck Run, a stream with the only documented brook trout population in the Deer Creek sub-watershed. The sub-watershed contains some of the best instream and riparian habitat in north-central Pennsylvania. The property is also within the Moshannon State Forest Landscape Conservation Area, a Clearfield County Natural Heritage Inventory site of approximately 254,000 acres considered one of the largest blocks of contiguous forest remaining in Pennsylvania.

sites.name: Rupert Cave
sites.description: Around 2011, George and Karen Bange contacted ClearWater about conserving the natural surface environment of their property as a method of protecting the underlying cave. The property was purchased by the Banges in two portions in 1996 and 2000. It contains 38 primarily forested acres, with approximately 4 acres of wetland and stream, 30 acres of upland habitat, and 4 acres of former agricultural field. The property contains approximately 1,220 feet of Town Run, a High Quality Cold Water Fishery, and is within the Exceptionally Significant Rupert Cave Biological Diversity Area as classified by the Mifflin County Natural Heritage Inventory of 2007. The property contains more than 7,000 feet of cave passages and a multitude of subterranean formations among the finest examples in Pennsylvania.

sites.name: Rockview
sites.description: Following lengthy discussions, a bill passed the Pennsylvania House and Senate in 2007 calling for divestiture of all lands belonging to SCI Rockview north of the I-99 corridor. A public outcry over ecological and cultural resources of Spring Creek Canyon tabled the legislation. Successor legislation House Bill 1890, signed as Act 55 of 2010, conveyed three parcels (A, B, and C totaling 451 acres) to Penn State University. Formerly used for the Rockview Farm Operation, the property consists of approximately 400 acres of agricultural land and 40 acres of woodland. It buffers a larger parcel containing three unnamed tributaries to Spring Creek and 3.3 miles of Spring Creek, a High-Quality Cold Water Fishery and Class A Wild Trout Stream. The adjacent Spring Creek Valley BDA is described as one of the most exceptional areas in Centre County for native biodiversity, with globally rare species.

sites.name: Rockview (Facilitated Conveyances)
sites.description: As part of the Rockview project, ClearWater helped facilitate the conveyance of 25.85 acres to Benner Township, 85.83 acres to the Pennsylvania Fish and Boat Commission, and 1,212.85 acres to the Pennsylvania Game Commission.

sites.name: Hale
sites.description: ClearWater acquired the Hale property and transferred ownership to DCNR Bureau of Forestry for addition to Rothrock State Forest. The five tracts are located along Tussey Mountain on the ridge facing State College, occupying a large portion of the indenture in Rothrock State Forest between the Musser Gap tract and Mountain Road to the east. Acquisition helped Ferguson and Harris Township realize long-term goals established by the Ridge-Overlay Zoning District (2007), enhanced recreational corridors from Musser Gap toward Shingletown Gap, contributed to long-term protection of the One-Year Zone of Contribution for the Harter and Thomas Well Fields, protected the Tussey Mountain viewshed, and protected Audubon's 'Greater Tussey Mountain' Important Bird Area.

sites.name: Decker
sites.description: PA DCNR purchased this property from the William Decker Estate for addition to Bald Eagle State Forest. The property includes 570 acres total, with a 465-acre woodland parcel and a 105-acre farmette. DCNR Bureau of Forestry Central Office and Bald Eagle State Forest worked with ClearWater to place a sales option on the property; ClearWater transferred the option to DCNR when acquisition funding became available. The woodland shares a 2.1-mile common boundary with Bald Eagle State Forest, and acquisition enables access to the Green Valley District Block from the south face of Nittany Mountain for the first time.

sites.name: Hoy
sites.description: With support from ClearWater, the Pennsylvania DCNR Bureau of Forestry purchased this property from Thomas Hoy for transfer to Rothrock State Forest.

sites.name: Mierley Oak Manor Farm
sites.description: When Oscar Mierley acquired the farm in the 1930s he named it 'Oak Manor' for a large oak tree (believed to be several hundred years old) just west of the house that still stands on the bank of a perennial tributary to Standing Stone Creek. Around 2009, owners John and Judy Mierley contacted ClearWater about conservation options. In 2014, the Mierleys completed an approximately 27-acre Wetland Reserve Program Easement through the Huntingdon County NRCS office. By 2016, the Mierleys and ClearWater executed a conservation easement on most of the remaining land. Approximately half of Oak Manor Farm is agricultural fields and the rest is mixed deciduous and coniferous forest. The farm contains a circa 1841 farmhouse, bank barn, and auxiliary buildings. PA State Route 26 bisects the farm providing scenic vistas. The property contains approximately 9,600 linear feet of tributaries to Standing Stone Creek, a High Quality Cold Water Fishery.

sites.name: Chicory Lane Farm
sites.description: John and Catherine Smith purchased Chicory Lane Farm in 1974 and are committed to preserving the property's capacity for self-renewal as a Biotic Community. The property is traversed by a perennial stream and an intermittent stream totaling 3,834 feet functioning as unnamed tributaries to Penns Creek, a High Quality Cold Water Fishery. The Smiths have largely re-vegetated the property — once fallow farm fields and overgrazed pastures are now 15 acres of mixed deciduous forest, 16 acres of native grasslands, and extensive wildflower meadows. The property also contains two restoration wetlands, a wet meadow, a cat-tail marsh, a constructed farm pond, and two small vernal pools.

sites.name: Everhart Farm
sites.description: The conservation concept for the Everhart Farm and adjacent Meyer Dairy properties originated with ClearWater's former Executive Director Jennifer Shuey around 2014. Located on the Regional Growth Boundary line and wedged between urban development and the Harter Thomas wellfields (the region's most productive drinking water wells), ClearWater launched the Slab Cabin Run Initiative in October 2016 to raise local awareness and funds. Acreage of 300.4 is the combined total for both the Everhart Farm and Meyer Dairy easements. Ultimately, funds were secured to acquire easements on both properties before Meyer Dairy acquired the Everhart Farm to continue dairy operations.

sites.name: Meyer Dairy
sites.description: Conservation easement acquired in conjunction with the adjacent Everhart Farm. See Everhart Farm (CE018) for full project description. The 300.4-acre figure reflects the combined acreage of both the Meyer Dairy and Everhart Farm properties.

sites.name: Nittany Noll Preserve
sites.description: A historic property acquired in the late 19th century by J.A. Noll and William Henry Noll of Pleasant Gap. In 1905, William Henry Noll co-founded Whiterock Quarries; his partner Noah H. Swayne was known as the closest living relative of George Washington at the time. The preserve sits above the Valentine seam of chemically pure limestone, which was mined for 50 years until Whiterock Quarries sold to American Marietta in 1955. Ray C. Noll Jr., a Life Member of ClearWater and Pennsylvania Forest Steward, began conservation discussions with ClearWater in the late 1990s. Talks went inactive but were reinitiated in Fall 2015 as negotiations with Graymont (PA) moved forward for a possible sale. Graymont accepted inclusion of a Conservation Easement on the surface estate, which became finalized with Graymont's January 31, 2017 acquisition. The preserve encompasses important waterways, forested habitats, subterranean features, hiking paths, scenic views, and historic places.

sites.name: Plummer's Hollow
sites.protection_type: Facilitated Conservation Easement (held by Western Pennsylvania Conservancy)
sites.description: Located just outside Tyrone, encompassing the entire watershed of Plummer's Run, a small mountain tributary draining Plummer's Hollow into the Little Juniata River before flowing to the Chesapeake Bay. With support from ClearWater, Marcia and Bruce Bonta donated a conservation easement on the property to the Western Pennsylvania Conservancy.

sites.name: Ellenberger
sites.description: With support from ClearWater, the Pennsylvania DCNR purchased the property from the Ellenberger estate et al for transfer to the Bureau of Forestry Rothrock State Forest on the north side of Tussey Mountain.

sites.name: Smith Trust
sites.description: In 2016, ClearWater staff learned that Donna Hughes was the executor of her family trust (Smith Trust) and was selling an 18.7-acre landlocked property immediately adjacent to State Game Lands 176 (Scotia Barrens). ClearWater had completed a stream restoration on the Hughes Farm in 2013. Donna expressed a preference for ClearWater to purchase the parcel so it could be conserved in perpetuity. ClearWater purchased the property and transferred it to the PA Game Commission for addition to SGL 176.

sites.name: Canoe Creek Conservation Bank
sites.description: In 2019, ClearWater finalized a permanent conservation easement in partnership with Magnolia Land Partners (Bank Sponsor) and U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service on a 214-acre property along the Frankstown Branch of the Juniata River, immediately across the river from Canoe Creek State Park, adjacent to PA Game Lands #147, and along the Lower Trail. This was one of the first such conservation bank projects on the east coast specifically focused on landscape-scale conservation of habitat for the federally endangered Indiana Bat (Myotis sodalist) and the federally threatened northern long-eared bat (Myotis septentrionalis). The site provides high quality forested habitat for summer maternity colonies near known bat hibernacula, and has been designated as High-Quality Forest Habitat in the 2017 Indiana Bat Compensation Protocol based on presence of high-value roost tree species such as Shagbark Hickory and Slippery Elm.

sites.name: Windy Hill Farm
sites.description: Conservation discussions with owners Brent and Cindy Pasquinelli began in 2005. In June 2020, ClearWater purchased a conservation easement on Windy Hill Farm for drinking water source protection (includes 1,228 feet of High Quality Slab Cabin Run), wetland preservation (an 11.7-acre wetland complex monitored by the Keystone Water Resource Center since 2005), and scenic open space for an important public recreation gateway (highly visible from Shingletown Road and the adjacent Musser Gap Greenway).

sites.name: Dry Hollow
sites.description: ClearWater Conservancy acquired this property, formerly owned by Dry Hollow Hunting Club, then transferred it to the Bureau of Forestry to be added to Rothrock State Forest, where it will be permanently conserved and publicly accessible. Of the 1,271 total acres, 905 fall within Warriors Mark Township, Huntingdon County — making this the first publicly accessible land in that township. The land is sometimes called 'Scotia West' due to its proximity to the Scotia Barrens, the largest remaining patch of pitch pine-scrub oak barrens in Pennsylvania. Iron ore exploration remnants from the 1800s, including an old railway, are still visible. Below the surface lies a large groundwater recharge area contributing to regional drinking water supplies and supporting some of the best trout fishing in the country as water flows toward Spruce Creek.

sites.name: Bella Vista Farm
sites.description: After learning about ClearWater's Slab Cabin Run Initiative, property owner Joe Dionisio contacted ClearWater with interest in placing a conservation easement on Bella Vista Farm. As an initial step, the property owners worked with ClearWater and partners to establish a several-acre multi-functional riparian buffer; tree and shrub planting occurred in fall 2020 with additional meadow planting starting spring 2021. The property contains approximately 1,150 linear feet of Slab Cabin Run flowing west to east across the southern portion, an approximately six-acre planted multi-functional riparian buffer and native meadow along Slab Cabin Run, and approximately 475 linear feet of road frontage along State Route 45 (Shingletown Road) providing public scenic views.

sites.name: Gray's Woods Preserve
sites.description: Located within the Scotia Barrens Biological Diversity Area (a PA Natural Heritage Inventory Core Area) and surrounded on three sides by State Game Lands 176. The Scotia BDA is where the Gatesburg geologic formation surfaces in Centre County, characterized by sandy well-drained soils, dominant pitch pine and scrub oak, and depressional wetlands. Patton Township acquired the land after voters approved a 2014 referendum to borrow $3,500,000 for open space preservation, supplemented by a $750,000 Hamer Foundation grant. The township purchased the land in December 2017 for $4,250,000 from Gray's Woods Partnership. In early 2022, ClearWater Conservancy and Patton Township executed the 'Declaration of Public Trust and Covenants and Grant of Conservation Easement' ensuring the property is conserved and open for public access in perpetuity.

sites.name: Dave Litke
sites.protection_type: Facilitated Conservation Easement (held by Centre County Farmland Trust)
sites.description: In December 2021, the Centre County Farmland Trust contracted ClearWater to help facilitate a donated conservation easement project on Dave Litke's farm in Bellefonte.

sites.name: Rockenbeck
sites.description: In 2022, ClearWater purchased 8.78 acres from the Rockenbeck Family in Houserville, including a farmhouse, bank barn, and a stretch of Spring Creek that represented one of ClearWater's first riparian plantings. In 2024, ClearWater subdivided and purchased an additional 1.73 acres from the family to restore 10.51 total acres and develop the Hamer Community Conservation Center.

sites.name: Shirley Long
sites.description: When Shirley Long passed away in February 2021, she left her property in her will to the Western Pennsylvania Conservancy to be conserved. The forested property sits at the watershed divide between the Frankstown Branch of the Juniata River watershed and the Little Juniata River watershed above Altoona on the Allegheny Front. The Western Pennsylvania Conservancy and ClearWater coordinated with the estate to place a permanent conservation easement on the property held by ClearWater.

sites.name: Snyder
sites.description: William Snyder, who routinely fishes Spring Creek, contacted ClearWater in 2021 about donating a conservation easement on his property in Little Sugar Valley. The property is on the dividing ridge of Nittany Mountain and comprised of high gradient clearwater creeks, mature oaks and hemlocks, and steep rocky slopes. It is part of the Roaring Run Biological Diversity Area, a designated PA Natural Heritage Site.

sites.name: Colerain Mansion
sites.description: The Colerain Forges Mansion is a regionally and nationally significant historic site in the Spruce Creek Valley. The property was jointly owned by a nonprofit formed in 2019 (the Colerain Center for Education, Preservation, and the Arts) and the same family that has owned the mansion since the 1980s. The owners contacted ClearWater about donating a conservation easement. The property contains a significant stretch of Warriors Mark Run, just upstream from its confluence with Spruce Creek, and was the site of a USFWS in-stream restoration around the time of the conservation easement.

sites.name: Joanne Fisher
sites.protection_type: Facilitated Conservation Easement (held by Centre County Agricultural Preservation Program)
sites.description: ClearWater, the Halfmoon Township Open Space Program, and the Centre County Agricultural Land Preservation Program collaborated together to conserve the Fisher Farm. ClearWater also funded a forest management plan.

sites.name: Ridge Farm
sites.protection_type: Facilitated Conservation Easement (held by Natural Lands; ClearWater named as Beneficiary)
sites.description: ClearWater, the Centre County Farmland Trust, and Natural Lands collaborated to conserve Steve and Betsy Wojdylak's Ridge Farm. Natural Lands obtained funding from the Open Space Institute and contracted ClearWater to prepare the Baseline Report. Natural Lands is also contracting ClearWater to conduct annual monitoring visits and act as the local steward.

sites.name: Albright
sites.description: The mostly wooded Albright property is located along Beaver Branch Creek in Pennsylvania Furnace. A portion of the Beaver Branch Biological Diversity Area, a Pennsylvania Natural Heritage Site, is located on the eastern portion of the property. Diane Albright worked with ClearWater, funded by the Open Space Institute, to permanently protect the property with a conservation easement.

sites.name: Joel's Woods
sites.protection_type: Conservation Easement, Trail Easement, and Fishing and Boating Access Easement
sites.description: Jamie and Sonia Felker purchased Joel's Woods from their friend Joel Myers, a retired agronomist and Myer Farm owner, then worked with ClearWater to permanently conserve the property and preserve Joel's conservation legacy. The wooded property is located outside Spring Mills on Egg Hill, contains a significant stretch of Sinking Creek, and encompasses a portion of the Sinking Creek Prairie Biological Diversity Area, an identified Pennsylvania Natural Heritage Area. With funding from the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation, Pennsylvania DCNR, and the Foundation for Pennsylvania Watersheds, ClearWater acquired a permanent conservation easement, a public fishing and boating access easement, and a public trail easement on a small section of the abandoned Lewisburg to Tyrone Rail Bed (identified in the Centre County Recreation and Greenway Plan).

sites.name: Daryabar
sites.description: On the edge of the Spring Creek Watershed, a conservation easement was donated on the Daryabar property (owners wish to remain anonymous). The steep, rocky, wooded property is located on Bald Eagle Mountain and contains an unnamed tributary to Buffalo Run. In addition to the conservation easement, a Public Trail Easement was established on a 12-acre section to share the incredible north and south views from the top of the ridge.
